one_line_takeaway: MINT Demo 2 reframes membership inference as a cooperative white-box auditing tool and packages it into a multimodal (face + LoRA-fine-tuned LLM) web platform, reporting up to ~89-90% detection accuracy on faces and AUC up to 0.9901 on LLMs, beating MinK/MinK++/Loss baselines.
key_finding: Membership can be detected with high accuracy in a realistic (non-'toy') white-box auditing setting. Image: CNN MINT reaches ~0.89 accuracy (Conv Layer #1) and Vanilla MINT reaches 0.84 (all conv layers concatenated, 100K samples), which the paper rounds to 'up to 90%'. Text (AUC): the All3Layers fusion is consistently best, reaching 0.9901 for DeepSeek-V3 on both CAMEL Math and Maths & College Reasoning; all layer-based MINT variants outperform the MinK/MinK++/Loss baselines. Deeper layers carry stronger membership signal (Qwen3 on Medical-o1 rises 0.8306->0.9272 from L1->L3), gradient-only features are weak (as low as 0.5927 for LLaMA 3.2-3B on Medical-o1) but recover strongly when combined with loss/confidence (DeepSeek-V3 Medical-o1: 0.6683->0.9435). Larger models (DeepSeek-V3) show higher, more stable AUC, suggesting stronger memorization.
core_contribution: The paper presents the Membership Inference Test (MINT) Demo 2, a framework and public web platform (ai-mintest.org) for auditing whether specific data samples were used to train an AI model. The authors reformulate Membership Inference Attacks (MIA) into an *auditing* tool: unlike MIA, which trains 'shadow models' to emulate a target it cannot access, MINT positions the operator as an authorized auditor with direct (white-box) access to the original model M, letting them train a MINT model T on 'Auxiliary Auditable Data' (intermediate activations) plus model outcomes to classify a sample as belonging to the training set D or an external set E. Demo 2's own novelty over the prior MINT Demo is the extension from image-only to a multimodal (image + text/LLM) framework, integrating MINT, aMINT, and gMINT technologies behind one platform. Note: the theoretical MINT formulation, the aMINT/gMINT variants, and the face/LLM MINT applications are attributed to the authors' own prior works ([10],[19],[20],[21],[22],[42],[43]); this paper's distinct new artifact is the consolidated multimodal demonstrator plus the LoRA-adapted LLM formulation and its evaluation.
threat_model: The privacy/governance concern addressed is the unauthorized or unconsented use of personal or copyrighted data to train AI models, motivated by the EU AI Act's transparency and auditing obligations. Rather than an adversary, the actor is an authorized auditor (or the platform, cooperating with a model developer via API) who has white-box access to the audited model M and a private external set E, and wants to detect whether a given sample d was in the training set D. The paper explicitly distinguishes this cooperative auditing setting from the adversarial, no-access MIA setting.
limitations: 1) MINT fundamentally requires white-box access and, in the platform, active developer cooperation (the developer supplies model M and training samples D via API); it is an auditing tool, not an adversarial capability, so its detection numbers are not comparable to zero-access MIA. 2) The evaluation uses only self-selected external sets and a single face model / four LLMs on three text datasets, with no comparison against an independent facial-recognition MIA (the authors note a 'dearth' of such studies). 3) CNN MINT cannot process the model-outcome vector or concatenate multi-resolution layers, limiting its feature fusion. 4) Reporting inconsistency: the contributions claim '6 diverse image databases with more than 22M images' and '6 large-scale text datasets,' but the reported experiments (Tables I-III) use Glint360k + 4 external image databases and only 3 text datasets. 5) This is a short demo paper; several core methods are inherited from the authors' prior work rather than introduced here.

datasets_used: Glint360k (17M images, face-recognition training set D)
quantitative_results: Vanilla MINT image accuracy up to 0.84 (all conv layers concatenated, 100K training samples; Table I)
CNN MINT image accuracy up to 0.89 (Conv Layer #1, 50K/100K samples; Table II) - described as 'almost 90%'
100K MINT-training samples = 0.3% of the face model's 17M training set
Text AUC best = 0.9901 (All3Layers, DeepSeek-V3, on CAMEL Math and on Maths & College Reasoning; Table III)
Layer-depth effect: Qwen3 Medical-o1 L1->L3 = 0.8306 -> 0.9272; DeepSeek-V3 Medical-o1 L1->L3 = 0.9697 -> 0.9846
LLM feature vector = 15 dimensions (12 activation + 1 gradient + 2 output)
LoRA fine-tuning config: rank r=32, alpha=32, dropout 0.15, ~13k samples
Prior-art 'toy' MIA accuracies cited for context: ~65% (CIFAR-10), ~73% (CINIC-10), ~80% (CIFAR-100)
baselines_compared: MinK++ / Min-K%++ (Zhang et al., MIA baseline)
Vanilla MINT MLP vs CNN MINT (internal architecture comparison for images)
MINT feature configurations: L1, L2, L3, OnlyGrad, GradProbLoss, All3Layers
